key_work.title: Letters to Arralla
key_work.credit_line:
Key art, concept art & stills from Letters to Arralla

Little Pink Clouds, Australia, 2024

Courtesy Little Pink Clouds
key_work.brief_description:
Letters to Arralla takes players to a whimsical island with pink skies, rolling hills and fruit n’ veggie villagers. As a mail delivery-turnip, players can explore the island, help neighbours and snoop into their business by reading their mail.

Letters to Arralla presents a cheeky dreamworld inspired by wistful memories of childhood imagination. Its charming playscape and soft plush characters cherish an idyllic form of traditional ‘adorable’ femininity. ‘Cosy’ and ‘wholesome’ indie games have flourished in recent years. They offer a refreshing take on videogame conventions and appeal to players who don’t want violence and competition and instead value gentle interactions and positive game worlds. This game reproduces the soft, warm textures reminiscent of the comfort of a beloved stuffed toy.

Letters to Arralla is currently under development and is supported by VicScreen.

end.brief_description: In the year 1999, a young woman leaves her quaint seaside house and returns to the city, leaving in the mailbox a card for the next owner, with instructions to forward any mail of hers to the new address. His reply, which he slips into the mailbox, finds its way to her, beginning a parallel-time love story separated by a span of two years.

results.description: From cottagecore inspired videogames to unsettling props, explore how dollhouses have been used and represented across screen culture.

key_work.title: My Dream PC
key_work.credit_line:
Key art, concept art & stills from My Dream PC

CODE RMIT, Australia 2025

Courtesy CODE RMIT
key_work.brief_description:
My Dream PC celebrates the women who build personal computers (PC) and prove that femininity and technical competency are complementary.

Inspired by feminine internet trends like ‘cottagecore’, players can use flowers, furniture and magic to design their own PC. Through the visual language of dollhouses and everyday objects, My Dream PC challenges the masculine, utilitarian image of the computer.

Computers are often linked to function and rationality; design principles that contrast with feminine stereotypes like decoration and sentimentality, and Apple’s virtual assistant Siri. By embracing hyper-femininity, My Dream PC celebrates the craft, tech-competency and creativity of women PC builders around the world.

results.description: Rummage through this assortment of props and antiques in horror on screen, discovering valuable connections between them.

key_work.title: Mystiques
key_work.credit_line:
Key art, concept art & stills from Mystiques

Lemonade Games, Australia, 2025

Courtesy Lemonade Games
key_work.brief_description:
Mystiques is a haunted antiquing game about four chaotic women who turn to psychic powers to save their failing shop. Inspired by the quirky antique stores in the Blue Mountains and internet-famous grifters like Anna Delvey and Elizabeth Holmes, Mystiques is both a whimsical paranormal adventure and a girlcore inventory game.

To keep the store alive, players rummage through the belongings of the recently deceased, bargaining with spirits to find the most valuable relics to sell. Lemonade Games’ debut mixes spooky adventure with vintage flair, echoing films like Suspiria (1977) and Jennifer’s Body (2009). It’s about making money, dealing with ghosts, and maybe texting your ex (though probably not a great idea).

Mystiques is supported by Screen Australia and is currently under development.

constellations_other.description: Tilda Swinton name has become synonymous with androgyny since she first appeared on our screens in the mid-1980s, leading a cultural shift towards today’s understanding of gender as fluid and ever-changing. Orlando was key to establishing her influence and allure.

constellations_other.description: Films about demonic possession use architecture and environment to heighten fear. Late Night with the Devil places evil in a 1970s TV studio, connecting works where sets, buildings and media spaces shape horror, tension and supernatural menace.

key_work.title: Kamata
key_work.credit_line:
Key art, concept art & stills from Kamata

STEMtoto Studio, Australia, 2025

Courtesy STEMtoto Studio
key_work.brief_description:
Clean up the vibrant island of Zimzemba as a young girl named Nia, uncovering its natural beauty and those responsible for its pollution.

Society tends to hold young girls as symbols of morality and hope for the future. While activists like Greta Thunberg are highly visible, Kamata amplifies the voices of young African girls, who often lack representation in videogames and on the political stage.

The game offers an Afrofeminist critique of the imbalances of responsibility and power between young African girls, their community, and the corporations whose ecological footprint threatens to compromise their future.

Developers STEMtoto (‘Mtoto’ is a Swahili term for ‘child’) develop games for future learning, and cultural and ecological regeneration.

Kamata is currently under development.

end.title: The Royal Tenenbaums [DVD]
end.creator_credit: Wes Anderson, Barry Mendel, American Empirical Pictures and Touchstone Pictures
end.brief_description: Three children - all precocious geniuses in their youth - struggle with the trials of adulthood in this eccentric black comedy from Wes Anderson, writer and director of “Bottle rocket” and “Rushmore”. In their pre-teens these three suffered the departure of their corrupt father, Royal, disintegrating familial harmony. Twenty-two years later a remorseful and estranged Royal seeks to return to the fold, on the pretext that he has terminal cancer. Now adults, the siblings feel like abject failures at life and are beset by excessive emotional problems, principally emanating from Royal’s off-hand mistreatment of them. The characters that occupy this fantastical world are imbued with an aching melancholia not dissimilar in tone to the writings of J.D. Salinger. Indeed the Tenenbaum children can’t help but resemble the precociously talented yet tragically dysfunctional Glass siblings of Salinger’s iconic text, each living with thwarted and frustrated desires. Utilising a series of vignettes and by presenting surreal, exaggerated interiors, saturated with character history and nuance, Anderson continues his exploration of the themes of the outsider, family, and the difficult transition to adulthood. This enchanting film is a finely detailed and mannered affair that whilst not eliciting uproarious laughs, successfully amuses in a wry, offbeat and endearingly eccentric manner. In an era when the Farrelly brothers and Adam Sandler dominate the comedy genre, Anderson represents a left-of-field auteur with a delightfully skewed perspective on notions of narrative, entertainment and cinema. Stellar cast includes: Gene Hackman, Anjelica Huston, Ben Stiller, Gwyneth Paltrow, Luke Wilson, Owen Wilson, Danny Glover and Bill Murray.

end.brief_description: Time is running out for Lola (Franka Potente). She’s just received a frantic phone call from her boyfriend Manni (Moritz Bleibtreu), who’s lost a small fortune in cash belonging to his Mob boss. Now she has to run for his life, to try and come up with the money before Manni pays the ultimate price for his mistake. Using a destiny device, the story begins anew with different outcomes. results.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
results.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

key_work.title: Immersive sound in cinema: THX
key_work.brief_description:
When Star Wars hit cinemas in the late 1970s and 80s, sound engineer Tomlinson Holman noticed a problem: the rich, detailed audio created during production wasn’t being heard properly in theatres. His solution was THX – a new sound standard that ensured films played exactly as filmmakers intended.
When Return of the Jedi (1983) premiered, only THX-certified cinemas could show it. Audiences were greeted by the now-iconic ‘Deep Note’ – a rising wave of tones swelling into a mighty D major chord. It shook the room and set the stage for the galaxy far, far away – as well as a new level of excellence in cinema sound.
Fun fact: THX was named after George Lucas’s first film, THX 1138 (1971).

end.creator_credit: Richard Marquand, Howard Kazanjian and Lucasfilm, Ltd.
end.brief_description: The conclusion of the “Star Wars” trilogy begins with the rescue of Hans Solo from the evil clutches of Jabba the Hutt. The Galactic Empire has resurrected the Death Star and plans to eliminate the Rebel Alliance once and for all. From the seedy den of Jabba the Hutt, to the luscious green peacefulness of the planet Endor with its Ewoks, “The Return of the Jedi” is an entralling action-packed special effects adventure.

end.title: DOOM
end.credit_line: Courtesy id Software and ZeniMax Media Inc
end.brief_description:
Doom (1993) is widely considered one of the most influential videogames of all time and is credited with inspiring the ‘first-person shooter’ (FPS) genre.

In 1996 the game’s developers, id Software, released the source code for Doom under a non-commercial license, to encourage porting, modding, and the creation of new games.

Before game engines were commercially available, most games were made with bespoke engines – the software framework used to create a game – or created as mods of existing engines.

The release of the source code not only extended the life of the game itself, but it also gave rise to an entirely new medium – the creative challenge of getting Doom running on anything. The blog It Runs Doom catalogues the unlikely devices that can run the game, from vintage videogame consoles to a digital camera to a tractor interface.

It Runs Doom

In 1997 id Software released the source code for their landmark game Doom (1993), making it free for use by anyone. The elegant simplicity and low hardware requirements of the code means that it runs on an extremely wide range of technology.

In this display you can see the work of Wētā Workshop graphics engineer James Brown, who got Doom running on a tiny 3D-printed toy block. Brown’s device is powered not by a battery, but by conductive bricks which were first designed for LEGO’s monorail set.

From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

links.description: Doom pioneered stereo and digital audio in games, using positional sound to immerse players. Like Jurassic Park, its sound design creates spatial awareness and tension, showing that immersive audio is essential not only in film but in interactive, gameplay-driven environments.

results.name: Haunted spaces
results.description: Films about demonic possession use architecture and environment to heighten fear. Late Night with the Devil places evil in a 1970s TV studio, connecting works where sets, buildings and media spaces shape horror, tension and supernatural menace.

key_work.title: Set design in Late Night with the Devil
key_work.brief_description:
Local horror film Late Night with the Devil (2023) is set on the fictional 1970s late-night talk show Night Owls with Jack Delroy. The film, shot as found footage during a Halloween special, follows spiritualists and sceptics – until things go horribly wrong, thanks to an uninvited supernatural guest.

Production Designer Otello Stolfo worked with the directors to accurately recreate the look of 1970s talk shows. From his original sketch shown here to the set’s palette of browns, oranges and blues, plus the patterns, finishes and dressings, Stolfo carefully recreated the style, tone and feel of Australian classics like The Don Lane Show (1975–83) and Countdown (1974–87), the latter of which he had worked on.

Filming in a Melbourne TV studio let the team use real floors and backstage areas, which added extra realism.

start.title: The Shining
start.creator_credit: Stanley Kubrick and Producer Circle Company
start.brief_description: In Stanley Kubrick’s chilling adaptation of a Stephen King horror novel, Jack Torrance, a struggling writer, accepts a job as caretaker of the Overlook Hotel, a luxurious but remote resort with a macabre history of violence. The hotel is closed for the winter, and Jack, his wife, Wendy, and son, Danny, will be the only residents for several snowy months. Jack hopes that the solitude will give him a chance to focus on his writing, but he soon spirals helplessly towards madness and murder as the hotel’s power plays upon his weaknesses. Cast includes: Jack Nicholson, Shelley Duvall, Scatman Crothers and Danny Lloyd.

start.title: Set design in Late Night with the Devil
start.creator_credit: Future Pictures and Otello Stolfo
start.brief_description:
Local horror film Late Night with the Devil (2023) is set on the fictional 1970s late-night talk show Night Owls with Jack Delroy. The film, shot as found footage during a Halloween special, follows spiritualists and sceptics – until things go horribly wrong, thanks to an uninvited supernatural guest.

Production Designer Otello Stolfo worked with the directors to accurately recreate the look of 1970s talk shows. From his original sketch shown here to the set’s palette of browns, oranges and blues, plus the patterns, finishes and dressings, Stolfo carefully recreated the style, tone and feel of Australian classics like The Don Lane Show (1975–83) and Countdown (1974–87), the latter of which he had worked on.

Filming in a Melbourne TV studio let the team use real floors and backstage areas, which added extra realism.

end.title: The Exorcist
end.creator_credit: William Friedkin, William Peter Blatty and Warner Bros Pictures
end.brief_description: The Exorcist occupies a central position in the history of horror films, an almost sadistically malevolent experience in terror. Regan (Linda Blair) is a young girl entering puberty and she becomes possessed by the spirit of an ancient demon. Her mother (Ellen Burstyn) finds that there is no assistance in the scientific pragmatism of the modern world and she hesitantly turns to the occult rites of Catholicism and Orthodoxy in order to rid her daughter of the monster. The exorcism scenes attain a disturbing authenticity and they are based on the official Catholic Church rites of exorcism. “The Exorcist” is a film that feeds on our most intimate fears and terrors and not a film for the easily frightened. Stars Max Van Sydow as Father Merrin. Based on the book by William Peter Blatty. Mercedes McCambridge is the voice of the Devil. Part of Tubular Bell by Mike Oldfield used on the soundtrack.

end.title: The Shining
end.creator_credit: Stanley Kubrick and Producer Circle Company
end.brief_description: In Stanley Kubrick’s chilling adaptation of a Stephen King horror novel, Jack Torrance, a struggling writer, accepts a job as caretaker of the Overlook Hotel, a luxurious but remote resort with a macabre history of violence. The hotel is closed for the winter, and Jack, his wife, Wendy, and son, Danny, will be the only residents for several snowy months. Jack hopes that the solitude will give him a chance to focus on his writing, but he soon spirals helplessly towards madness and murder as the hotel’s power plays upon his weaknesses. Cast includes: Jack Nicholson, Shelley Duvall, Scatman Crothers and Danny Lloyd.

results.name: Regeneration
results.description: Terra Nil reimagines city-building as world-healing. Across games, films and animation, creators explore renewal instead of conquest – asking how we might restore balance, rebuild damaged ecosystems, and live alongside nature rather than above it.

key_work.title: Terra Nil
key_work.brief_description:
Terra Nil (2023) is an antidote to city builder and strategy games.

While many city-builder games focus on exploiting environmental resources, colonising land and unstainable expansion, Terra Nil asks players to bring a wasteland back to life by restoring nature and building healthy ecosystems.

The game asks: ‘What if we put nature first?’

The creators, Free Lives, were inspired by their home in Cape Town, South Africa. They based parts of the game on local scrublands, which are small but important ecosystems, which are under constant threat. They also looked at real conservation efforts happening nearby.

Terra Nil uses real ideas from environmental science, like rewilding and controlled burning to fight climate change. The game encourages players to consider new possibilities for a sustainable future. A percentage of the games profit is donated to a non-profit wildlife organisation.

end.title: Flower
end.brief_description:
ESCAPE TO A DREAMLIKE WORLD

From the studio behind the genre-bending titles Journey and flOw comes Flower, a unique gaming experience that lets you soar through a dreamlike world of luscious fields and rolling hills. Control the wind to carry a stream of flower petals around each landscape and watch as it comes to life.

Every level has its own unique aims and little mysteries – but you don’t have to worry about them if you don’t feel like it. Flower is about doing what you want, when you want. Freedom is everything.

constellations_other.name: David Dalaithngu
constellations_other.description: David Dalaithngu was a Yolngu man of the Mandhalpuyngu speech of the Djinba language. As a young boy, Dalaithngu was an accomplished hunter, tracker and dancer, which he turned into an acclaimed film career that saw him win the Best Actor award at the Cannes Film Festival (for Charlie's Country) and an AACTA for The Tracker, among his many honours.

constellations_other.name: Painterly vision
constellations_other.description: The Australian New Wave transformed landscapes and interiors into expressive, painterly spaces. The Devil’s Playground exemplifies this, showing how light, composition, and texture shape mood, narrative and character, connecting films that transform cinema into visual art and emotional storytelling.

links.description: Nicolas Roeg’s Walkabout turns the Australian outback into a vivid, painterly tableau. Like Picnic at Hanging Rock, its cinematography emphasises texture, light, and scale, presenting landscape as both aesthetic and narrative force, central to the New Wave’s exploration of environment and mood.

start.title: Walkabout
start.creator_credit: Nicolas Roeg, Si Litvinoff and Max L. Raab - Si Litvinoff Films
start.brief_description: Nicolas Roeg’s lyrical tale about a sister and her young brother left stranded in the Australian outback having survived their father’s attempt to destroy the family. They’re rescued by a young Aboriginal man (David Gulpilil) who has journeyed into the vast desert on his walkabout - a tribal initiation into manhood.

end.title: The Last Wave [DVD]
end.creator_credit: Peter Weir, Hal McElroy, James McElroy and Ayer Productions
end.brief_description: In this atmospheric mystery thriller a young Sydney lawyer, while defending four First Nations men against a murder charge, is troubled by dreams and is drawn into an underground world, the intimations of an ancient civilization. Step by step a series of apparently random occurrences assume a disturbing pattern. The city becomes a facade for a place of ancient ritual. Cast includes Richard Chamberlain and David Gulpilil and features photography by Russell Boyd. Features a digitally restored soundtrack.

links.description: Peter Weir’s The Last Wave blends urban interiors with rural landscapes to evoke mystical tension. Like Walkabout, it treats Australian locations as cinematic brushstrokes, combining naturalism with expressive lighting to create painterly compositions that communicate mood, mythology and spiritual unease.

links.description: Gillian Armstrong’s My Brilliant Career uses sunlit landscapes and carefully composed interiors to reflect character and emotion. Like The Last Wave, its cinematography transforms environment into expressive tableaux, continuing the New Wave’s painterly approach and its connection between Australian landscape and narrative.

end.title: Bright Star
end.creator_credit: Jane Campion, Janet Patterson, Alexandre de Franceschi, Caroline Hewitt, Jan Chapman, Bright Star Films, Hopscotch Productions, Jan Chapman Productions, New South Wales Film & Television Office, Pathe, Screen Australia, UK Film Council and Andrew Motion
end.brief_description: In 1818, high-spirited young Fanny Brawne finds herself increasingly intrigued by the handsome but aloof poet John Keats, who lives next door to her family friends the Dilkes. After reading a book of his poetry, she finds herself even more drawn to the taciturn Keats. Although he agrees to teach her about poetry, Keats cannot act on his reciprocated feelings for Fanny, since as a struggling poet he has no money to support a wife.

links.description: Jane Campion’s Bright Star employs meticulous framing, natural lighting, and textured interiors, echoing Australian New Wave sensibilities. Like My Brilliant Career, it uses a painterly cinematography to evoke emotion and character, connecting landscapes and domestic spaces in visually expressive ways.

end.title: Days of Heaven
end.creator_credit: Terrence Malick, Harold Schneider, Siobhan Bowers and Paramount Pictures
end.brief_description: This visually sumptuous film is the story of three young slum dwellers who are forced to leave Chicago and search for work in the wheat fields of Texas. It is the turn-of-the-century and the depression has created enormous poverty. Bill (Richard Gere) forms a plan to inherit the fortune of his employer, and secure a future for his girlfriend (Brooke Adams) and sister (Linda Manz). This sets in train a chain of deceit and betrayal which leads to tragedy. The cinematography of the stark Texas landscape is by Academy Award winning Nestor Alemandros. The film also stars writer Sam Shepard. Critically “Days of Heaven” has been acclaimed as a leading film of the 1970’s Hollywood “new wave”.

links.description: Terrence Malick’s Days of Heaven, with its sun-drenched landscapes and precise compositions, demonstrates a global parallel to the painterly approach of the Australian New Wave.
